chunk_summary.idea_trajectory_justification: Participants primarily share their individual research backgrounds and introduce new concepts (like mRNA modifications) to expand the space of potential research questions, rather than committing to a single direction.
chunk_summary.collective_engagement_justification: Multiple non-speaking participants display visible head nods and attentive facial expressions while others are sharing their research ideas.
chunk_summary.cross_disciplinary_bridging_description: Connected mRNA modifications (biochemistry) to viral mutation rates and host-parasite interactions.
chunk_summary.problem_specificity_justification: The group progressed from general topics to a specific hypothesis: targeting host mRNA modification enzymes in a sequence-dependent manner could prevent viral spillover without causing host toxicity.
chunk_summary.decision_crystallization_justification: A specific project idea (targeting host mRNA modification enzymes) was named and actively explored by multiple participants, though no formal commitment was made.
chunk_summary.funding_reference_description: Wilfred explicitly mentioned the goal of writing proposals and how funders generated the discussion prompts.
chunk_summary.notable_observation: The group successfully navigated a minor logistical hiccup regarding incorrect discussion prompts and smoothly transitioned into substantive scientific ideation, with Kristin's introduction of mRNA modifications serving as a strong anchor for subsequent cross-disciplinary exploration.

ideas_currently_on_table: Modeling vector-borne disease patterns and climate dependence
Genetic variants for host colonization success
Targeting host mRNA modification enzymes to prevent viral spillover
chunk_summaries: The group began the session with Wilfred van der Donk outlining the process and initiating a round of introductions. Each participant shared their disciplinary background and current research focus, ranging from RNA virology and biophysical modeling to parasite-host interactions and horizontal gene transfer. The tone was polite and structured, and the chunk ended with Kristin Koutmou volunteering to be the reporter before the group transitioned to discussing the prompts.

The group spent the majority of the chunk conducting structured introductions, with each participant sharing their background and expertise. Facilitator Rebecca Garabed then shared her screen to display the discussion prompts, giving the group time to read them silently. In the final minute, Silvie Huijben initiated the substantive discussion by proposing a focus on modeling vector-borne diseases and their dependence on climate.

The group navigated a minor logistical issue with the discussion prompts before transitioning into substantive scientific ideation. Participants shared their individual research interests, with Kristin Koutmou introducing the novel concept of mRNA modifications affecting viral mutation rates. This sparked cross-disciplinary exploration, leading Wilfred van der Donk to propose targeting these host modification enzymes to prevent viral spillover, an idea the group began to critically evaluate.
